title: Chikkanagappa vs Anuradha on 22 August, 2013
body: Chikkanagappa vs Anuradha on 22 August, 2013 Author: L.Narayana Swamy Bench: L. Narayana Swamy IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA, AT BANGALORE DATED THIS THE 22ND DAY OF AUGUST 2013 BEFORE THE HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE L. NARAYANA SWAMY REGULAR FIRST APPEAL NO.284 OF 2010 Between: 1. Shri. Chikkanagappa S/o Late Munithimmaiah Aged about 82 years 2. Shri. Muniraja S/o Chikkanagappa Aged about 46 years Both residing at Agraga Sarjapur Road Begur Hobli Bangalore South Taluk 560 102. 3. Smt. Yashodamma W/o Shri Venkatesh Aged about 48 years Residing at No. 60/71/A10 Begur Road Near MMR Traders, Hosur Krishnagiri District Tamilnadu 635 001. 4. Smt. Manjula W/o Shri Satish Aged about 34 years Residing at Gramadevatha Cross, Chikkamavalli No.C1, Near Ganesha Temple 2 Bangalore 560 004. ....Appellants (By Shri M.S.Venugopal for A.Krishna Bhat, Advocate) AND 1. Smt. Anuradha W/o Late Krishnappa Aged about 37 years 2. Kumari Swetha D/o Late Krishnappa Aged about 15 years, Minor Represented by her mother respondent no.1 as guardian (Amended as per court order dt.18.10.12) Both residing at No.4 Janatha Colony,Jigani Anekal Taluk 562 106. 3. Shir Venkatappa S/o Late Munithimmaiah Dead by LRs a) Smt. Somakka W/o Late Narayanappa Aged about 60 years b) Smt. Munilakshmamma W/o Chandrappa Aged about 50 years Both are residing at No.79 24th Main 6th Cross Sarjapura Road, Agara Bangalore 560 102. c) Smt. Muniyammam - Deleted 3 vide court order dt. 12/1/11 d) Smt. Jayamma W/o Shri Marappa Aged about 52 years Residing at No.212, II Cross Swamy Vivekananda Road Agara, Sarjapur Road Bangalore 560 102. 4. Shri. Dasappa - deleted As per order dated 29.7.2008 5. Shri. Muniswamappa S/o Shri Venkatappa, Major 6. Shri. Manjappa S/o Shri Dasappa, Major All are residents of Agara Sarjapura Road, Begur Hobli Bangalore South Taluk 560 102. ....Respondents ( By Shri. L.Venkatarama Reddy, Advocate for R1 R2, R3 (A)-(D); R5; R6 are served & unrepresented R4 & R3 (c) are deleted) This Regular First Appeal is filed under Section 96 CPC against the judgment and decree dated 18.2.2009 passed in OS No.758 of 1997 on the File of the XXXVIII Addl. City Civil and Sessions Judge Bangalore, partly decreeing the suit for partition and separate possession and future mesne profits; and etc., This appeal coming on for Hearing, this day, the Court made the following: JUDGMENT 4 The defendants No.1, 4, 7 and 8 is OS No. 758 of 1997 on the file of the XXXVIII Additional City Civil & Sessions Judge, Bangalore are the appellants herein. Respondents no.1 and 2 are the plaintiffs in the suit filed for partition and separate possession. The Trail Court, by its order dated 18th February 2008 decreed the suit partly holding that the suit schedule property A, B, C and D, and item No.2 of schedule 'D' are joint family property. This appeal has been filed by the defendants confining their prayer only in respect of defendant No.2 of Schedule 'D' property, which is a site bearing No.58 situated in Agara village of Begur Hobli, Bangalore (South) Taluk measuring 30 x 50 feet. Plaintiffs- Respondents No.1 and 2 herein, have contended that the very property is a joint family property, in which they are entitled to a share. The suit has been decreed in respect of the very property holding that item No.2 of Schedule 'D' is a joint family property. It is the case of the appellant- defendant that this property was granted to defendant No.1 and hence to be treated as self-acquired property. 2. The grounds urged by the appellant-defendant are that the plaintiffs 1 and 2 have not pleaded anything nor an iota of 5 contention raised. To substantiate the claim in respect of this property, no materials have been produced and absolutely there is no evidence. The additional issue No.2 framed by the Court below shifts the burden on the plaintiffs to prove the status of the property. Since, the plaintiffs had utterly failed by not proving that this particular property is a joint family property, the additional issue no.2 should have been answered in favour of the defendants. Since, there was no issue framed against the defendants, the shifting of burden to prove the status of property, is an error on the part of the Court below by allowing the suit, particularly, holding that the suit schedule property is a joint family property in spite of the evidence of PW2 to the effect that her late husband has put up the construction on the site granted in respect of defendant No.1. For the purpose of holding that the suit schedule property is a joint family property, condition no.4 in the grant certificate should not have been relied upon by the Court. In support of this submission, the learned counsel relied upon the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in the case of KUPPALA OBUL REDDY v. BONALA VENKATA NARAYANA REDDY THROUGH LRs reported in AIR 6 1984 SC 1171, wherein at paragraph No.15 of the judgment it has been held that "there may be presumption that there is a Hindu Joint Family but there can be no presumption that the joint family possesses joint family properties." No issues with regard to the properties being a joint family property was raised and no such issue could possible have been raised in the absence of pleading to that effect. 3. Per contra, the learned counsel for the plaintiff- respondents herein, submits that schedule 'D' of item No.1 of the suit schedule property is a joint family property. Though an application was made by the defendant No.1, but the grant would be made to the family and not to an individual. When the relation-ship between the parties is not in dispute and the union of the family is also not in dispute, then a presumption is to be drawn that the grant made to the kartha of the family is for the entire family and hence the family members cannot be excluded for the purpose of partition. With regard to the contention raised by the learned counsel for the appellant as regards additional issue No.2 is concerned, the learned counsel submits that the burden of proof is shifted on the plaintiffs to prove that the site 7 is a self acquired property or a joint family property and the said issue has been answered by relying on Exhibit D2 the grant certificate. When the grant certificate is a conclusive proof granted in favour of the kartha of the family, it is for the benefit of family members. The defendants also have not produced the application filed for granting the land in order to assess whether it was granted to an individual or to the family. Under these circumstances, the legal presumption has to be drawn in favour of the plaintiffs. In support of this submission, the learned counsel referred the judgment in the case of K.V. SUDARSHAN v. A. RAMAKRISHNAPPA AND OTHERS reported in 2008 AIR SCW 7419 and referred to paragraphs 13 and 14 of the judgment. Hence, the learned counsel submits to dismiss the appeal. 4. Heard both. The suit was filed in respect of four items of property, of which item No.2 of Schedule 'D' is relevant in the instant case. This property came to the respondent No.1 by way of grant under Ashraya Scheme. The land husband of the plaintiff and defendant No.4 are the sons of defendant No.1. It has come in the deposition of PW1 that her late husband put up a house. The said evidence has come in the form of affidavit 8 evidence. The defendant has produced Exhibit D2, the grant certificate and except that she has not produced the application, if any, made by defendant No.1 in an individual capacity. Specially, in the beneficial schemes like 'Ashraya', the sites will be allotted to the site-less families. 'Family' means and includes all persons including children, parents, etc. When such is the object in allotting the sites under 'Ashraya' scheme, it ha to be treated that the allotment was made to the family and not to an individual. When plaintiff has filed affidavit stating that her late husband contributed in putting up the construction, the same has not been rebutted by any evidence. The plaintiff is widow and she deposed on her own and not produced any material. However, in the partition suit the parties therein have claimed on the strength of exhibit D2 and except that there is no evidence and materials. Under these circumstances, it is relevant to refer to the judgment in the case of K.V. SUDHARSHAN (supra) wherein at paragraph 14 of the judgment, it has been observed thus: "14. ... it would be wrong to hold that simply because the conditions in Section 6A were fulfilled, the respondent 9 No.1 was granted occupancy rights and it was his individual rights. The truth is that the respondent No.1 became the Archak after the death of his father because he was the eldest in the family and only then came the question of satisfying the conditions of Section 6A ." In the light of the judgment, it clarifies the position that Exhibit D2-the grant certificate is made in favour of defendant No.1-appellant herein is not in his individual capacity and it is to his family and the late husband of the plaintiff is a member of the family as a son and his interest could not be excluded. Hence, the plaintiff is also entitled for the benefit. Under these circumstances, the reasons assigned by the Court below is sound and proper and there are no infirmities in the judgment dated 18th February 2009 passed in OS No.758 of 1997. Accordingly, the appeal is dismissed. Sd/- JUDGE lnn
